# Same secret-merge conflict flow, but with the sesam dir nested under sub/
# rather than at the worktree root. Git runs the merge driver from the worktree
# root ($WORK) with the baked-in --sesam-dir=sub, so the driver's sesam dir is
# NOT its cwd. This guards that the paths handed to `git merge-file` are absolute
# (rooted at the sesam dir); with sesam-relative paths git merge-file would look
# under $WORK/.sesam/tmp instead of $WORK/sub/.sesam/tmp and fail.
#
# Hooks are intentionally NOT installed here: the post-checkout aggressive clean
# currently corrupts a nested repo (it compares worktree-relative index names
# against sesam-relative walk paths, so it deletes the tracked .gitignore /
# .gitattributes / sesam.yml, after which the revealed plaintext stops being
# ignored). Skipping hooks keeps the repo sane so this test isolates the merge
# driver's path handling. With .gitignore intact, sub/secret.txt is never tracked
# and the ONLY thing that can write conflict markers into it is the sesam driver.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

# Stash the content versions outside the worktree and drop the originals: a
# nested layout has no .gitignore at the worktree root, so leaving them here
# would get them committed by `git add -A`.
cp base.txt $TMPDIR/base.txt
cp ours.txt $TMPDIR/ours.txt
cp theirs.txt $TMPDIR/theirs.txt
rm base.txt
rm ours.txt
rm theirs.txt

# Init the sesam repo under sub/ with the merge driver but without hooks.
mkdir sub
exec sesam init --user admin --sesam-dir sub --install-merge --install-hooks=false

# The driver command carries --sesam-dir=sub (git runs it from the worktree root).
exec git config --local merge.sesam-merge-secret-sub.driver
stdout 'sesam --sesam-dir=sub merge secret'

exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Base version XB of the secret, added and committed from inside sub/.
cd sub
cp $TMPDIR/base.txt secret.txt
exec sesam add secret.txt --group admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'add secret (XB)'

# Branch OURS: XB -> XO.
exec git checkout -b ours
cp $TMPDIR/ours.txt secret.txt
exec sesam seal
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'ours: XO'

# Branch THEIRS branches off the XB commit (ours~1) and changes it to XT.
exec git checkout -b theirs ours~1
cp $TMPDIR/theirs.txt secret.txt
exec sesam seal
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'theirs: XT'

# Merge THEIRS into OURS. Both sides changed the same line: the driver must run
# across the nested boundary, decrypt, and write conflict markers into sub/secret.txt.
exec git checkout ours
! exec git merge theirs

# sub/secret.txt is gitignored (never tracked), so these markers can only come
# from the sesam merge driver resolving the nested tmp paths correctly.
grep '^<<<<<<< ' secret.txt
grep '^=======$' secret.txt
grep '^>>>>>>> ' secret.txt
grep 'secret = O' secret.txt
grep 'secret = T' secret.txt

# The object itself must be recorded as conflicted (unmerged), proving the
# driver - not git's default text merge - handled it. Paths in `git status` are
# worktree-relative, so the nested object carries the sub/ prefix. The revealed
# sub/secret.txt stays gitignored and must not appear.
exec git status --porcelain
stdout '^UU sub/\.sesam/objects/secret\.txt\.sesam$'
! stdout 'sub/secret\.txt'

-- base.txt --
line one
secret = B
line three

-- ours.txt --
line one
secret = O
line three

-- theirs.txt --
line one
secret = T
line three
